---
title: "When You Do Not Know the SD: the t-Distribution and Small-Sample Intervals"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Statistical Inference"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20240611)

DATA   = "#2b3a67"     # samples / histograms / the t-curve
NORMAL = "#c1121f"     # the Gaussian reference curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / exact values / coverage hits
GREY   = "#6c757d"

def normal_pdf(t, mu=0.0, sigma=1.0):
    """The Gaussian density — the n -> infinity limit the t-curve relaxes onto."""
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((t - mu) / sigma) ** 2) / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article in the **Interval
> Estimation** movement. It assumes [INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals: What "95% Confident"
> Actually Means*], which built the **coverage** definition (the interval is random, the
> parameter is fixed) and the **pivotal-quantity** recipe for turning a known sampling
> distribution into an interval, then ran that recipe on the one case where everything is
> clean: a Normal mean with **known** variance, giving $\bar X_n \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,
> \sigma/\sqrt n$. It also leans on [INFR-A2 *LLN and the CLT*] for the sampling
> distribution of $\bar X_n$ and on [INFR-A1 *The Estimator as a Random Variable*] for the
> standard-error idea. The honest gap C1 left open is the one every real dataset hits: **you
> never know $\sigma$.** This article closes it. We build the **chi-square distribution of
> the sample variance** from scratch (we need it here and again in [INFR-D2]), assemble the
> **Student t-distribution** as a Normal divided by a scaled chi-square root, derive its
> density, and from it the small-sample interval for a mean. We extend to **two samples**
> and the **Welch** correction for unequal variances, and we close at the edge — non-normal
> small samples where the t-interval's coverage quietly fails, pointing forward to the
> bootstrap [INFR-E2]. It **sets up** [INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*] (the one- and
> two-sample t-tests are this exact machinery turned into a decision) and stands as the
> assumption-laden foil to the assumption-light bootstrap.
>
> It imports three results by name, each built from scratch in the Probability track and
> used here only by its statement: the **Normal and chi-square distributions** and that a
> sum of squared independent standard Normals is chi-square [PROB-14]; the **Gamma function
> and Gamma density** that the chi-square is a special case of [PROB-15]; and a
> **change-of-variables formula for densities** [PROB-11]. Everything above the Calculus-I
> floor that those carry is flagged at the point of use.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

The confidence interval $\bar X_n \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma/\sqrt n$ from [INFR-C1] has a
fatal practical defect: it contains $\sigma$, the population standard deviation, which we
never know. The obvious fix is to plug in the sample standard deviation $s$ — but $s$ is
*itself* a noisy estimate, and pretending it is the truth makes the interval too short, so
it covers less often than advertised. This article repairs the interval exactly. We show
that when the data are Normal, the quantity $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$ follows a **chi-square
distribution** with $n-1$ degrees of freedom — which we build from the ground up,
including the non-obvious fact that $\bar X_n$ and $s^2$ are independent — and that
$\bar X_n$ and $s^2$ combine into a pivot, the **t-statistic** $T = (\bar X_n - \mu)/(s/\sqrt
n)$, whose distribution is a Normal divided by the square root of a scaled independent
chi-square. We derive the density of that distribution — **Student's $t$ with $n-1$ degrees
of freedom** — in full, read its fatter tails as the price of estimating $\sigma$, and
show analytically that it relaxes back onto the Gaussian as $n\to\infty$. The payoff is the
small-sample interval $\bar X_n \pm t_{n-1,\,\alpha/2}\, s/\sqrt n$, which we extend to the
two-sample problem and the Welch correction. The single claim the article defends: **the
$t$ is not a different idea from the $z$, it is the same pivot honestly accounting for one
more estimated quantity** — and the moment the data stop being Normal in a small sample,
even that honesty is not enough, and you reach for the bootstrap.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be independent draws from $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ with **both**
$\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown. Estimate the mean by $\bar X_n=\frac1n\sum X_i$ and the
variance by the sample variance $s^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i-\bar X_n)^2$. Then the
studentized pivot
$$
T \;=\; \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{s/\sqrt n}
$$
does **not** follow a standard Normal — it follows **Student's $t$-distribution with
$n-1$ degrees of freedom**, a symmetric bell curve like the Normal but with **heavier
tails**, because the denominator $s$ is itself random and occasionally small, blowing $T$
up. The honest confidence interval for $\mu$ is therefore
$$
\boxed{\;\bar X_n \;\pm\; t_{n-1,\,\alpha/2}\,\frac{s}{\sqrt n}\;}
$$
with $t_{n-1,\alpha/2}$ the upper-$\alpha/2$ quantile of that $t$ distribution — *wider*
than the Normal interval, by exactly the amount needed to pay for not knowing $\sigma$. As
$n\to\infty$ the extra width vanishes and $t\to z$: with enough data, estimating $\sigma$
costs nothing.
:::

Everything orbits that box. The whole article is the answer to one question: *what is the
exact distribution of $T$ when we have replaced the known $\sigma$ by the estimated $s$?*
The replacement looks innocent, but it converts a clean Normal pivot into something
genuinely new, and getting the interval right means getting that something right —
exactly, not approximately, when the data are Normal.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the t {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the entire article: **when we do not know $\sigma$ and must
estimate it from the same small sample, how much wider must the interval be to stay
honest?** Every derivation below is an answer to some piece of that.

## What [INFR-C1] gave us, and the crack in it {#sec-crack}

[INFR-C1] built the confidence interval for a Normal mean by the **pivot** method. The key
object was the standardized mean
$$
Z \;=\; \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n},
$$ {#eq-z-pivot}
which — when the $X_i$ are Normal, or by the CLT [INFR-A2] when $n$ is large — is a
*standard Normal*, $\mathcal N(0,1)$. The defining feature of a pivot is that its
distribution does **not** depend on the unknown parameter: $Z$ is $\mathcal N(0,1)$ no
matter what $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are. That is what let us write
$P(-z_{\alpha/2}\le Z\le z_{\alpha/2}) = 1-\alpha$ and algebraically rearrange the
inequality to trap $\mu$:
$$
\bar X_n - z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\; \mu \;\le\;
\bar X_n + z_{\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.
$$ {#eq-z-interval}

Read @eq-z-interval and find the lie. It contains $\sigma$ — the population standard
deviation. In the entire history of applied statistics, the number of times an analyst
knew $\sigma$ but not $\mu$ is essentially zero: if you knew how variable the population
was, you would almost certainly know its center too. Knowing $\sigma$ is a classroom
fiction. **The interval as written cannot be computed from data.**

## The tempting fix, and why it is not free {#sec-tempting}

The fix everyone reaches for first: estimate $\sigma$ from the sample. The natural estimate
is the **sample standard deviation** $s=\sqrt{s^2}$, with
$$
s^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\bar X_n)^2 .
$$ {#eq-s2-def}
(The $n-1$ rather than $n$ is the unbiasedness correction; [INFR-A1] derived
$\mathbb E[s^2]=\sigma^2$ with this divisor, and @sec-chisq below re-derives the same
fact as a by-product of a deeper result. We will explain the $n-1$ *mechanically* in
@sec-df.) Then just substitute $s$ for $\sigma$ and use the interval
$\bar X_n \pm z_{\alpha/2}\, s/\sqrt n$.

For large $n$ this is exactly right, and [INFR-A2] told us why: $s\to\sigma$ in
probability (LLN on the squared deviations), and **Slutsky's theorem** says replacing a
known constant in a converging-in-distribution sequence by something that converges to that
constant leaves the limit untouched. So $(\bar X_n-\mu)/(s/\sqrt n)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal
N(0,1)$, and for big samples plugging in $s$ is asymptotically free.

But "asymptotically free" is a promise about the limit. For a **small** sample it is not
free at all, and here is the intuition for why. We have replaced one fixed number,
$\sigma$, by a *random* one, $s$. On some samples $s$ comes out smaller than $\sigma$ —
and when it does, the denominator $s/\sqrt n$ is small, so the ratio
$(\bar X_n-\mu)/(s/\sqrt n)$ is *large*. The studentized quantity therefore takes extreme
values more often than a standard Normal would: its tails are **heavier**. If we keep using
the Normal quantile $z_{\alpha/2}=1.96$ as though nothing changed, our interval is too
short for how wild the ratio actually gets, and it covers the truth *less* than the
advertised $95\%$. @fig-undercoverage measures the damage directly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-undercoverage
#| fig-cap: 'The cost of pretending $s=\sigma$. From a true $\mathcal N(0,1)$ source we draw 20000 samples at each of several sizes $n$, and for each form the *naive* interval $\bar X_n \pm 1.96\,s/\sqrt n$ (using the Normal quantile but the estimated $s$). The bars show how often that interval actually covers the true mean $\mu=0$. At $n=2$ it covers only about 70%; the shortfall shrinks as $n$ grows but is still visible at $n=10$. The honest fix — using the wider $t$ quantile instead of 1.96 — restores coverage to the dashed 95% target exactly (green).'
ns = [2, 3, 5, 10, 30]
B = 20000
naive_cov, t_cov = [], []
for n in ns:
    samp = rng.standard_normal((B, n))
    xbar = samp.mean(axis=1)
    s = samp.std(axis=1, ddof=1)
    se = s / np.sqrt(n)
    naive = np.mean(np.abs(xbar) <= 1.96 * se)
    tq = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=n - 1)
    tcov = np.mean(np.abs(xbar) <= tq * se)
    naive_cov.append(naive); t_cov.append(tcov)

xpos = np.arange(len(ns))
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(xpos - 0.2, naive_cov, width=0.4, color=NORMAL, label=r"naive: $\bar X \pm 1.96\,s/\sqrt{n}$")
ax.bar(xpos + 0.2, t_cov, width=0.4, color=ACCENT, label=r"$t$: $\bar X \pm t_{n-1}\,s/\sqrt{n}$")
ax.axhline(0.95, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label="target 95%")
ax.set_xticks(xpos); ax.set_xticklabels([f"n={n}" for n in ns])
ax.set_ylabel("actual coverage"); ax.set_ylim(0.6, 1.0)
ax.set_title("Using 1.96 with estimated $s$ undercovers at small $n$")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
for n, nc in zip(ns, naive_cov):
    print(f"n={n:>2}: naive coverage = {nc:.3f}")
```

The naive interval is not a rounding error at small $n$ — at $n=2$ it covers barely $70\%$
of the time while claiming $95\%$. That gap is the entire reason the $t$-distribution
exists.

## The history: a brewer with a sample of four {#sec-history}

The distribution is named for "Student," the pen name of **William Sealy Gosset**, a
chemist and statistician at the Guinness brewery in Dublin who published it in *Biometrika*
in 1908. Guinness's problem was the small-sample problem in its purest form. To monitor the
quality of barley, hops, and yeast, Gosset could afford only a handful of measurements —
sometimes as few as four. The large-sample Normal theory of the day (Karl Pearson's world)
was useless to him: with $n=4$ the estimate $s$ of the variability is itself wildly
uncertain, and treating it as known gave intervals and tests that were dangerously
overconfident — exactly the undercoverage of @fig-undercoverage.

Gosset's contribution was to work out the *exact* distribution of $(\bar X-\mu)/(s/\sqrt
n)$ for finite $n$ under Normal data, accounting for the randomness of $s$ in the
denominator. (Guinness forbade employees from publishing under their own names, lest
competitors learn the brewery used statistics — hence "Student.") Ronald Fisher, a few
years later, supplied the rigorous derivation via the geometry of $n$-dimensional Normal
samples and connected it to the chi-square distribution; that is essentially the
derivation we give below. The lesson Gosset forced onto statistics is the *why* of this
whole article: **when the sample is small and $\sigma$ is estimated, you must pay for the
estimation, and the $t$-distribution is the exact bill.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just use the Normal and accept a little undercoverage — surely it washes out?"*
It does wash out — *asymptotically*, and we will prove $t\to z$ in @sec-limit. But the
regime where you do not know $\sigma$ is overwhelmingly the regime where $n$ is small (if
$n$ were huge you would know $\sigma$ essentially exactly). So the correction matters
exactly when you cannot afford to ignore it. The $t$ costs you nothing when $n$ is large
(the quantile is within a hair of $z$) and saves you from real overconfidence when $n$ is
small — there is no reason *not* to use it. The honest one-liner: *use $t$ whenever you
estimate $\sigma$; it can only help.*
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, hold three pictures.

**One: the denominator is now a random variable.** In the $z$-pivot @eq-z-pivot the
denominator $\sigma/\sqrt n$ is a fixed number. In the $t$-pivot it is $s/\sqrt n$, and
$s$ jitters from sample to sample. The $t$-statistic is therefore a ratio of *two* random
things — a Normal numerator and a random denominator — not a Normal rescaled by a constant.
A ratio of randoms is heavier in the tails than the numerator alone, because the two ways
to get a big ratio (big numerator *or* small denominator) both contribute.

**Two: degrees of freedom is "how much information is left to estimate spread."** We use
$n$ data points; one degree of freedom is spent locating the center $\bar X_n$, leaving
$n-1$ to measure spread around it. The fewer leftover degrees of freedom, the noisier $s$
is, the heavier the $t$'s tails. With $n-1$ huge, $s$ is essentially $\sigma$ and the $t$
is essentially Normal.

**Three: the $t$ is a Normal smeared by an independent random scale.** This is the precise
version of picture one, and it is the engine of the whole derivation. Write
$$
T \;=\; \frac{\bar X_n-\mu}{s/\sqrt n}
\;=\; \frac{(\bar X_n-\mu)\big/(\sigma/\sqrt n)}{s/\sigma}
\;=\; \frac{Z}{s/\sigma},
$$
where $Z=(\bar X_n-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$ is *exactly* the standard Normal from @eq-z-pivot,
and $s/\sigma$ is a random positive scale near $1$. So $T$ is a standard Normal divided by a
random number that is *usually* close to $1$ but sometimes noticeably less. @fig-ratio-intuition
shows what dividing a clean Normal by that wobbly scale does to the shape.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ratio-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Why the tails fatten. We draw $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ (the numerator) and an independent scale $W=s/\sigma$ for $n=4$ (so $s$ is very noisy), then histogram both $Z$ alone (red outline) and the ratio $T=Z/W$ (blue fill). Dividing by a scale that is often below 1 throws extra mass into the tails: the blue distribution has the same center and symmetry as the Normal but is visibly heavier far from zero. That heavier tail is the $t$-distribution, and it is the reason 1.96 is not enough.'
n_demo = 4
B = 200000
Z = rng.standard_normal(B)
# s/sigma for N(mu, sigma^2): (n-1) s^2 / sigma^2 ~ chi^2_{n-1}, derived in sec-chisq.
chi2 = rng.chisquare(df=n_demo - 1, size=B)
W = np.sqrt(chi2 / (n_demo - 1))
T = Z / W

grid = np.linspace(-6, 6, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.hist(T, bins=300, range=(-6, 6), density=True, color=DATA, alpha=0.55,
        label=r"$T=Z/(s/\sigma)$  (this is $t_3$)")
ax.hist(Z, bins=300, range=(-6, 6), density=True, histtype="step",
        color=NORMAL, lw=1.8, label=r"$Z\sim N(0,1)$")
ax.plot(grid, normal_pdf(grid), color=NORMAL, lw=1.0, alpha=0.6)
ax.set_xlim(-6, 6); ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"A Normal divided by a random scale has heavier tails")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"P(|Z|>3)  = {np.mean(np.abs(Z)>3):.4f}")
print(f"P(|T|>3)  = {np.mean(np.abs(T)>3):.4f}   <- much larger: fat tails")
```

The printed tail probabilities make picture one quantitative: $|T|$ exceeds $3$ several
times more often than $|Z|$ does. To turn these pictures into an exact interval we need the
exact distribution of the denominator $s$, which is the chi-square story, and then the
exact distribution of the ratio, which is Gosset's $t$.

# The distribution of the sample variance: chi-square from scratch {#sec-chisq}

Everything hinges on the distribution of $s^2$. We build it now. The destination is:

::: {#thm-chisq}
**Theorem (sampling distribution of $s^2$).** If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are independent
$\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, then
$$
\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma^2}
\;=\; \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\bar X_n)^2
\;\sim\; \chi^2_{\,n-1},
$$
a **chi-square distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom**, and moreover $\bar X_n$ and
$s^2$ are **independent**.
:::

We need three ingredients, each established below in its own subsection: what the
chi-square distribution *is* (@sec-chisq-def), the algebraic identity that splits the total
squared spread into a mean part and a variance part (@sec-split), and the geometric/
orthogonality argument that makes those two parts independent and counts the degrees of
freedom (@sec-orthogonal).

## What the chi-square distribution is {#sec-chisq-def}

::: {#def-chisq}
**Definition (chi-square).** If $Z_1,\dots,Z_k$ are independent standard Normals, the
distribution of
$$
Q \;=\; Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + \cdots + Z_k^2
$$
is the **chi-square distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom**, written $Q\sim\chi^2_k$.
:::

This is the *defining* fact we import from [PROB-14]: the sum of $k$ independent squared
standard Normals has a specific, named distribution. We will need its density and one
moment. Both follow from a single computation we do once here so nothing is left
floating — the $k=1$ density — and then a known closure property we flag.

**The $\chi^2_1$ density (derived).** Let $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Q=Z^2$. We find the
density of $Q$ by the change-of-variables technique (the cumulative-distribution method,
which needs only Calc-1). For $q>0$,
$$
F_Q(q) = P(Z^2 \le q) = P(-\sqrt q \le Z \le \sqrt q)
= \Phi(\sqrt q) - \Phi(-\sqrt q),
$$
where $\Phi$ is the standard Normal CDF. Differentiate in $q$ using the chain rule and
$\Phi'=\phi$ (the Normal density), with $\frac{d}{dq}\sqrt q=\frac{1}{2\sqrt q}$ and
$\frac{d}{dq}(-\sqrt q)=-\frac{1}{2\sqrt q}$:
$$
f_Q(q) = \phi(\sqrt q)\frac{1}{2\sqrt q} - \phi(-\sqrt q)\Bigl(-\frac{1}{2\sqrt q}\Bigr)
= \frac{1}{2\sqrt q}\bigl[\phi(\sqrt q)+\phi(-\sqrt q)\bigr].
$$
The Normal density is symmetric, $\phi(-u)=\phi(u)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-u^2/2}$, so
$\phi(\sqrt q)+\phi(-\sqrt q)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-q/2}$ (note $(\sqrt q)^2=q$). Hence
$$
f_{\chi^2_1}(q) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt q}\cdot\frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-q/2}
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,q^{-1/2}e^{-q/2},
\qquad q>0 .
$$ {#eq-chisq1}

This is a **Gamma density** in disguise. Recall the Gamma$(\alpha,\beta)$ density from
[PROB-15], $f(x)=\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}$, where the
**Gamma function** $\Gamma(\alpha)=\int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt$ generalizes the
factorial. Matching exponents in @eq-chisq1: $x^{\alpha-1}=q^{-1/2}$ gives $\alpha=\tfrac12$,
and $e^{-\beta x}=e^{-q/2}$ gives $\beta=\tfrac12$. So $\chi^2_1 = \text{Gamma}(\tfrac12,
\tfrac12)$, and the normalizing constant checks out because $\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt\pi$
(also from [PROB-15]): $\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}=\frac{(1/2)^{1/2}}{\sqrt\pi}
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$, exactly the constant in @eq-chisq1.

**The general $\chi^2_k$ density.** Gamma distributions with the same rate $\beta$ add their
shape parameters when independent variables are summed — that is, if $Q_1\sim\text{Gamma}
(\alpha_1,\beta)$ and $Q_2\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha_2,\beta)$ are independent, then
$Q_1+Q_2\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha_1+\alpha_2,\beta)$. This additivity is a standard
moment-generating-function result proved in [PROB-15]; we flag it as imported rather than
re-derive it here, because the MGF machinery lives in the Probability track. Summing $k$
independent $\chi^2_1 = \text{Gamma}(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$ pieces therefore gives
$\text{Gamma}(\tfrac k2,\tfrac12)$:
$$
f_{\chi^2_k}(q) = \frac{(1/2)^{k/2}}{\Gamma(k/2)}\,q^{k/2-1}e^{-q/2}
= \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}\,q^{k/2-1}e^{-q/2},
\qquad q>0 .
$$ {#eq-chisq-k}
We will need this density in @sec-t-density to build the $t$. We also note its mean for
later: since each $Z_i^2$ has $\mathbb E[Z_i^2]=\operatorname{Var}(Z_i)=1$, linearity gives
$\mathbb E[\chi^2_k]=k$. @fig-chisq draws the family.

```{python}
#| label: fig-chisq
#| fig-cap: 'The chi-square family $\chi^2_k$, the distribution of a sum of $k$ squared standard Normals (@eq-chisq-k). For $k=1$ the density diverges at 0 (it is $q^{-1/2}$ there); as $k$ grows the distribution shifts right (its mean is exactly $k$) and grows more symmetric, foreshadowing that for large degrees of freedom $s^2$ concentrates tightly around $\sigma^2$. The sample variance lives on this family: $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-1}$.'
q = np.linspace(0.01, 20, 500)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for k, c in zip([1, 2, 4, 8], [DATA, ACCENT, GREY, NORMAL]):
    ax.plot(q, stats.chi2.pdf(q, df=k), color=c, label=fr"$k={k}$ (mean ${k}$)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.55); ax.set_xlabel("$q$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"The $\chi^2_k$ family")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The algebraic split: total squared spread = mean part + variance part {#sec-split}

Now to $s^2$ itself. The numerator of $s^2$ is $\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)^2$. We relate it to
the simpler $\sum_i (X_i-\mu)^2$ — squared deviations from the *true* mean, which are
clean because each $(X_i-\mu)/\sigma$ is a standard Normal. The bridge is a pure algebra
identity. Insert and subtract $\bar X_n$ inside each term:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)^2
= \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl[(X_i-\bar X_n)+(\bar X_n-\mu)\bigr]^2 .
$$
Expand the square inside the sum with $(a+b)^2=a^2+2ab+b^2$, taking $a=X_i-\bar X_n$ and
$b=\bar X_n-\mu$ (note $b$ does not depend on $i$):
$$
= \sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)^2
\;+\; 2(\bar X_n-\mu)\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)
\;+\; \sum_i (\bar X_n-\mu)^2 .
$$
The middle sum vanishes: $\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n) = \sum_i X_i - n\bar X_n
= n\bar X_n - n\bar X_n = 0$, because $\bar X_n$ is by definition the value that makes the
deviations sum to zero. The last term is $(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$ summed $n$ times, i.e.
$n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2$. So
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\mu)^2
\;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\bar X_n)^2}_{\text{spread about }\bar X_n}
\;+\; \underbrace{n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2}_{\text{the mean's own deviation}} .
$$ {#eq-split}

Divide @eq-split through by $\sigma^2$ and read it as a statement about chi-squares:
$$
\underbrace{\sum_i \Bigl(\frac{X_i-\mu}{\sigma}\Bigr)^2}_{\textstyle A}
\;=\;
\underbrace{\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_i (X_i-\bar X_n)^2}_{\textstyle B \,=\, (n-1)s^2/\sigma^2}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\Bigl(\frac{\bar X_n-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}\Bigr)^2}_{\textstyle C} .
$$ {#eq-split-chisq}
(The last term rearranges as $n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2/\sigma^2 = \bigl((\bar X_n-\mu)/(\sigma/
\sqrt n)\bigr)^2$.) Now identify the pieces:

- **$A$** is a sum of $n$ independent squared standard Normals — each $(X_i-\mu)/\sigma$ is
  $\mathcal N(0,1)$ — so $A\sim\chi^2_n$ by @def-chisq.
- **$C$** is the square of $(\bar X_n-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)$. We know from [INFR-A1] that
  $\bar X_n\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$ exactly for Normal data, so that ratio is a
  *single* standard Normal, and $C\sim\chi^2_1$.
- **$B$** is the thing we want, $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$.

So @eq-split-chisq reads $\chi^2_n = B + \chi^2_1$. If we could show $B$ and $C$ are
independent, then $B$ would have to be a $\chi^2_{n-1}$ — a $\chi^2_n$ is a $\chi^2_{n-1}$
plus an independent $\chi^2_1$ (add an $(n-1)$-term sum of squares to a $1$-term one). The
degrees-of-freedom arithmetic $n = (n-1) + 1$ is the whole content of @thm-chisq. Everything
now rests on independence, which is the subtle part.

## Why $\bar X_n$ and $s^2$ are independent: orthogonality {#sec-orthogonal}

That $\bar X_n$ (which drives $C$) and $s^2$ (which is $B$) are independent is *not*
obvious — both are computed from the same data, and for non-Normal data it is generally
**false**. It is a special gift of the Normal distribution, and the cleanest argument is
geometric. We give it in full because it both proves independence and *counts* the degrees
of freedom, answering the "what is a degree of freedom" question mechanically.

Center the data: let $Y_i = (X_i-\mu)/\sigma$, so $Y=(Y_1,\dots,Y_n)$ is a vector of $n$
independent $\mathcal N(0,1)$'s. Two facts about this vector, both imported from [PROB-14]
and flagged here:

1. **Rotational symmetry of the standard Normal.** The joint density of $Y$ is
   $\prod_i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-y_i^2/2} = (2\pi)^{-n/2}e^{-\|y\|^2/2}$, which depends
   on $y$ *only through its length* $\|y\|^2=\sum y_i^2$. A density that depends only on
   length is unchanged by any rotation (orthogonal transformation) of the coordinates. So
   if $O$ is any orthogonal matrix, $OY$ has the same distribution as $Y$ — again $n$
   independent $\mathcal N(0,1)$'s. This is the defining geometric property of the standard
   multivariate Normal.
2. **Orthogonal pieces of a standard Normal vector are independent.** Because the
   coordinates of $OY$ are again independent standard Normals, any group of them is
   independent of any disjoint group. So if we split $\mathbb R^n$ into orthogonal
   subspaces and look at the components of $Y$ in each, those components are independent.

Now choose the rotation cleverly. Let $\mathbf 1=(1,1,\dots,1)/\sqrt n$ be the unit vector
along the diagonal. Decompose $Y$ into its component along $\mathbf 1$ and the part
orthogonal to $\mathbf 1$:
$$
Y \;=\; \underbrace{(Y\cdot\mathbf 1)\,\mathbf 1}_{\text{along }\mathbf 1}
\;+\; \underbrace{\bigl(Y-(Y\cdot\mathbf 1)\mathbf 1\bigr)}_{\text{orthogonal to }\mathbf 1}.
$$
Compute the projection coefficient: $Y\cdot\mathbf 1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt n}\sum_i Y_i
= \sqrt n\,\bar Y$, where $\bar Y=\frac1n\sum Y_i$. So:

- The **component along $\mathbf 1$** has squared length $(Y\cdot\mathbf 1)^2 = n\bar Y^2$.
  But $\bar Y = (\bar X_n-\mu)/\sigma$, so $n\bar Y^2 = n(\bar X_n-\mu)^2/\sigma^2 = C$ from
  @eq-split-chisq. This piece *is* the mean. It lives in a **1-dimensional** subspace
  (the line through $\mathbf 1$).
- The **orthogonal component** has squared length $\|Y\|^2 - (Y\cdot\mathbf 1)^2
  = \sum_i Y_i^2 - n\bar Y^2$. Writing $Y_i^2 = ((X_i-\mu)/\sigma)^2$ and using the split
  @eq-split-chisq, this equals $A - C = B = (n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$. This piece *is* the sample
  variance. It lives in the **$(n-1)$-dimensional** subspace orthogonal to $\mathbf 1$.

By fact 2, the projection onto the line (giving $\bar X_n$) and the projection onto the
orthogonal $(n-1)$-dimensional complement (giving $s^2$) are **independent**, because they
are the components of a rotated standard Normal vector in disjoint orthogonal subspaces.
That proves the independence claim of @thm-chisq.

And it counts the degrees of freedom *mechanically*: the variance piece lives in an
$(n-1)$-dimensional space, so it is a sum of squares of $n-1$ independent standard Normals
(pick any orthonormal basis of that subspace; the coordinates of $Y$ in it are $n-1$
independent $\mathcal N(0,1)$'s, and $B$ is the sum of their squares). Hence $B\sim
\chi^2_{n-1}$, completing @thm-chisq. @fig-chisq-empirical confirms it by simulation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-chisq-empirical
#| fig-cap: 'Confirming @thm-chisq. For Normal data with $n=6$ we draw 100000 samples, compute $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$ for each, and histogram it (blue) against the theoretical $\chi^2_{5}$ density (red). They match. The right panel scatters $\bar X_n$ against $s^2$ across samples: the cloud shows no tilt or funnel — the two estimates are uncorrelated, the visible signature of the independence the orthogonality argument proved (and which holds *only* because the data are Normal).'
n = 6
B = 100000
samp = rng.standard_normal((B, n))   # sigma=1, mu=0
xbar = samp.mean(axis=1)
s2 = samp.var(axis=1, ddof=1)
stat = (n - 1) * s2 / 1.0

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.hist(stat, bins=120, density=True, color=DATA, alpha=0.6, label="empirical $(n-1)s^2/\\sigma^2$")
qq = np.linspace(0.01, 25, 400)
axL.plot(qq, stats.chi2.pdf(qq, df=n - 1), color=NORMAL, label=fr"$\chi^2_{{{n-1}}}$ density")
axL.set_xlim(0, 25); axL.set_xlabel("value"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title(r"$(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-1}$"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)

axR.scatter(xbar[:3000], s2[:3000], s=5, color=DATA, alpha=0.25)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\bar X_n$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$s^2$")
axR.set_title(f"corr = {np.corrcoef(xbar, s2)[0,1]:+.3f}  (independent)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why exactly $n-1$ degrees of freedom — give me the mechanical reason, not just the
unbiasedness correction."* Because estimating the variance uses the deviations $X_i-\bar
X_n$, and those deviations are **not** free: they are forced to sum to zero
($\sum_i(X_i-\bar X_n)=0$, shown in @sec-split). One linear constraint removes one degree
of freedom from the $n$ data points, leaving $n-1$ truly free directions in which the data
can deviate from their own mean. Geometrically (this article's argument), the deviation
vector $X-\bar X_n\mathbf 1$ is *orthogonal* to the all-ones direction, so it lives in an
$(n-1)$-dimensional subspace and is a sum of squares of $n-1$ independent Normals. The
$n-1$ divisor in $s^2$ and the $n-1$ degrees of freedom are the *same* fact viewed two
ways: you spent one observation's worth of information locating the center.
:::

# Assembling the t-distribution {#sec-t-build}

We now have the two independent pieces. Combine them.

::: {#def-t}
**Definition (Student's $t$).** If $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Q\sim\chi^2_k$ are
**independent**, then
$$
T \;=\; \frac{Z}{\sqrt{Q/k}}
$$
follows **Student's $t$-distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom**, written $T\sim t_k$.
:::

The pivot is exactly this shape. From @sec-intuition, $T=(\bar X_n-\mu)/(s/\sqrt n)$ can be
written
$$
T \;=\; \frac{(\bar X_n-\mu)\big/(\sigma/\sqrt n)}{\,\sqrt{\,s^2/\sigma^2\,}\,}
\;=\; \frac{Z}{\sqrt{\,\dfrac{(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2}{\,n-1\,}\,}}
\;=\; \frac{Z}{\sqrt{Q/(n-1)}},
$$ {#eq-t-as-ratio}
where $Z=(\bar X_n-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Q=(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2\sim
\chi^2_{n-1}$ — and these two are **independent** by @thm-chisq. So by @def-t,
$$
\boxed{\;T = \frac{\bar X_n-\mu}{s/\sqrt n} \sim t_{\,n-1}.\;}
$$ {#eq-t-pivot}

Note the unknown $\sigma$ cancelled completely in @eq-t-as-ratio: it divides out of the
numerator and the denominator. **That cancellation is what makes $T$ a pivot** — its
distribution ($t_{n-1}$) depends on neither $\mu$ nor $\sigma$, only on the known sample
size. This is the same pivotal logic as [INFR-C1], now surviving the replacement of $\sigma$
by $s$ because of how the two pieces were built from the *same* $\sigma$.

## The density of the t-distribution {#sec-t-density}

We derive the $t_k$ density from @def-t — no "it can be shown." We have independent
$Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Q\sim\chi^2_k$, and want the density of $T=Z/\sqrt{Q/k}$.

The clean route is to first find the joint density of $(T,Q)$ via a change of variables
from $(Z,Q)$, then integrate $Q$ out. Define the transformation
$$
T = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{Q/k}}, \qquad Q = Q,
$$
i.e. we keep $Q$ and replace $Z$ by $T$. Solve for the old variables in terms of the new:
$Z = T\sqrt{Q/k}$ and $Q=Q$. The change-of-variables formula for densities ([PROB-11])
says $f_{T,Q}(t,q) = f_{Z,Q}\bigl(z(t,q),q\bigr)\,\bigl|\det J\bigr|$, where $J$ is the
Jacobian of $(z,q)$ with respect to $(t,q)$. Compute it:
$$
J = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial t} & \dfrac{\partial z}{\partial q} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} & \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial q}\end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{q/k} & \dfrac{t}{2\sqrt{kq}} \\[8pt] 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad |\det J| = \sqrt{q/k}.
$$
(The determinant is the product of the diagonal since the lower-left is $0$; only the
$\partial z/\partial t = \sqrt{q/k}$ entry survives.) Because $Z$ and $Q$ are independent,
the joint density factors: $f_{Z,Q}(z,q)=\phi(z)\,f_{\chi^2_k}(q)$ with $\phi(z)=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}$ and $f_{\chi^2_k}$ from @eq-chisq-k. Substitute $z=t\sqrt{q/k}$,
so $z^2 = t^2 q/k$:
$$
f_{T,Q}(t,q)
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2 q/(2k)}
\cdot \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}\,q^{k/2-1}e^{-q/2}
\cdot \sqrt{q/k}.
$$
Now integrate out $q$ over $(0,\infty)$ to get the marginal $f_T(t)$. Collect the powers
of $q$ — we have $q^{k/2-1}\cdot q^{1/2}=q^{(k+1)/2-1}$ — and the exponentials —
$e^{-t^2 q/(2k)}e^{-q/2}=\exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{q}{2}(1+t^2/k)\bigr)$. Pull constants out:
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)\sqrt k}
\int_0^\infty q^{(k+1)/2-1}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{q}{2}\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)\Bigr)\,dq .
$$ {#eq-t-integral}
The integral is a **Gamma integral**. Recall $\int_0^\infty x^{a-1}e^{-bx}\,dx
= \Gamma(a)/b^{a}$ for $a,b>0$ — this is just the definition of $\Gamma$ after the
substitution $u=bx$ (a Calc-1 substitution; we are flagging the Gamma-function identity as
imported from [PROB-15]). Here $a=\frac{k+1}{2}$ and $b=\frac12(1+t^2/k)$, so
$$
\int_0^\infty q^{(k+1)/2-1}e^{-bq}\,dq
= \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{b^{(k+1)/2}}
= \Gamma\!\Bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl[\tfrac12\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)\Bigr]^{-(k+1)/2}.
$$
Substitute back into @eq-t-integral. The factor $[\tfrac12]^{-(k+1)/2}=2^{(k+1)/2}$
combines with the $2^{-k/2}$ out front to give $2^{1/2}=\sqrt2$, which cancels the $\sqrt2$
hiding in $\sqrt{2\pi}=\sqrt2\sqrt\pi$:
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)\sqrt k}
\cdot 2^{(k+1)/2}\Bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-(k+1)/2}
= \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\pi k}\,\Gamma(k/2)}
\Bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-(k+1)/2}.
$$
So we have derived, with every constant accounted for:

::: {#thm-t-density}
**Theorem (Student's $t$ density).** The $t_k$ distribution has density
$$
f_{t_k}(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{k\pi}\;\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k}{2}\bigr)}
\Bigl(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}},
\qquad t\in\mathbb R .
$$
:::

Two features of this formula are the whole story. It is **symmetric** in $t$ (only $t^2$
appears), so the $t$ is centered and symmetric like the Normal. And its tails are a **power
law**, which we read straight off the density rather than assert. For large $|t|$ the
inside of the bracket is dominated by the $t^2/k$ term — $1+t^2/k \approx t^2/k$ — so
$$
f_{t_k}(t) \;\approx\; \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{k\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k}{2}\bigr)}
\Bigl(\tfrac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}}
= \underbrace{\frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\bigr)\,k^{k/2}}{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k}{2}\bigr)}}_{\text{a constant in }t}\;
|t|^{-(k+1)},
$$
using $(t^2)^{-(k+1)/2}=|t|^{-(k+1)}$ and pulling the $k$-powers into the constant. So the
density decays only **polynomially**, like $|t|^{-(k+1)}$, whereas the Normal density
$e^{-t^2/2}$ decays super-exponentially. A polynomial tail is *much* heavier than the
Normal's: that is the fat tail of @fig-ratio-intuition, now exact. The smaller $k$ is, the
slower the decay and the heavier the tail. @fig-t-family draws the family converging onto
the Normal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-t-family
#| fig-cap: 'Student''s $t_k$ density (@thm-t-density) for several degrees of freedom, with the standard Normal (black dashed) as the $k\to\infty$ limit. Every $t$ is symmetric and bell-shaped but has heavier tails and a lower peak than the Normal; the deficit is dramatic at $k=1$ (the Cauchy distribution, which has no mean) and already nearly invisible by $k=30$. The inset zooms the right tail near $t=3$, where the practical difference lives: the $t_2$ density there is several times the Normal''s, which is exactly why the $t$ quantile is larger.'
t = np.linspace(-5, 5, 600)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for k, c in zip([1, 2, 5, 30], [NORMAL, ACCENT, GREY, DATA]):
    ax.plot(t, stats.t.pdf(t, df=k), color=c, label=fr"$t_{{{k}}}$")
ax.plot(t, normal_pdf(t), color="black", ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"$N(0,1)$")
ax.set_xlim(-5, 5); ax.set_xlabel("$t$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title(r"$t_k \to N(0,1)$ as $k$ grows")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)

axins = ax.inset_axes([0.62, 0.45, 0.34, 0.4])
tt = np.linspace(2.5, 5, 200)
for k, c in zip([1, 2, 5, 30], [NORMAL, ACCENT, GREY, DATA]):
    axins.plot(tt, stats.t.pdf(tt, df=k), color=c)
axins.plot(tt, normal_pdf(tt), color="black", ls="--", lw=1.2)
axins.set_title("right tail", fontsize=8); axins.tick_params(labelsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The t relaxes onto the Normal as n grows {#sec-limit}

The Core promised $t\to z$ as $n\to\infty$. We prove it from @thm-t-density — analytically,
every step. Fix $t$ and let $k\to\infty$. Split the density into the constant prefactor and
the $t$-dependent bracket:
$$
f_{t_k}(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{k\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k}{2}\bigr)}}_{c_k}
\cdot \Bigl(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}} .
$$

**The bracket.** Take logs of the bracket and use the limit definition of the exponential
— the *one* limit from Calc 1 we lean on, $\lim_{k\to\infty}(1+a/k)^k=e^a$. Write
$$
\ln\Bigl(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}}
= -\frac{k+1}{2}\,\ln\Bigl(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\Bigr).
$$
For large $k$, $\ln(1+t^2/k) = \frac{t^2}{k} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{t^4}{k^2}+\cdots$ (the
Calc-1 Taylor expansion of $\ln(1+u)$ at $u=0$). Multiply by $-\frac{k+1}{2}$:
$$
-\frac{k+1}{2}\Bigl(\frac{t^2}{k}-\frac{t^4}{2k^2}+\cdots\Bigr)
= -\frac{t^2}{2}\cdot\frac{k+1}{k} + O(1/k)
\;\xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{}\; -\frac{t^2}{2}.
$$
Exponentiating, the bracket $\to e^{-t^2/2}$ — the kernel of the standard Normal density.

**The prefactor.** We must show $c_k\to\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$ so that the limit is the
*normalized* Normal. The one imported tool is **Stirling's approximation**
$\Gamma(z+1)\sim\sqrt{2\pi z}\,(z/e)^z$ as $z\to\infty$ (flagged as imported from [PROB-15]
rather than re-derived). The general fact we need is the ratio of two Gamma values a
half-step apart: $\Gamma(z+\tfrac12)/\Gamma(z)\sim\sqrt z$. Get it from Stirling in the
convenient form $\ln\Gamma(z)\sim (z-\tfrac12)\ln z - z + \tfrac12\ln(2\pi)$ by subtracting
the two log-Gammas (everything but the $\ln z$ terms cancels to leading order):
$$
\ln\Gamma\!\bigl(z+\tfrac12\bigr) - \ln\Gamma(z)
\sim \bigl(z\bigr)\ln\bigl(z+\tfrac12\bigr) - \bigl(z-\tfrac12\bigr)\ln z - \tfrac12
\;\xrightarrow{z\to\infty}\; \tfrac12\ln z,
$$
so $\Gamma(z+\tfrac12)/\Gamma(z)\sim\sqrt z$. Apply this with $z=\frac{k}{2}$, so
$z+\tfrac12=\frac{k+1}{2}$:
$$
\frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k}{2}\bigr)}
\sim \sqrt{\tfrac{k}{2}},
\qquad\text{hence}\qquad
c_k = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{k\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\frac{k}{2}\bigr)}
\sim \frac{\sqrt{k/2}}{\sqrt{k\pi}}
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}.
$$
Therefore $f_{t_k}(t)\to\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2/2}=\phi(t)$ pointwise. The $t$ density
converges to the standard Normal density. Equivalently and more intuitively: as $n\to\infty$
the denominator piece $s/\sigma\to 1$ (the LLN, @sec-tempting), the random scale in
@sec-intuition stops jittering, and the ratio $Z/(s/\sigma)$ becomes just $Z$. The fat
tails were entirely the price of a noisy $s$; with enough data $s$ is essentially $\sigma$
and the price drops to zero. This is the analytic content of the Slutsky argument from
[INFR-A2], now made exact at every finite $n$ by @thm-t-density.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"At what $n$ can I just use $z=1.96$ instead of the $t$ quantile?"* There is no magic
threshold (the "use $t$ below 30, $z$ above" rule of thumb is folklore, not a theorem).
Look at the quantiles directly: $t_{29,\,0.025}=2.045$ versus $z=1.960$ — about a 4% wider
interval at $n=30$, often negligible. At $n=10$ it is $t_9=2.262$, a 15% widening; at $n=5$
it is $t_4=2.776$, a 42% widening; at $n=2$ it is $t_1=12.71$, a sixfold blowup. The
correct answer is *always use $t$ when you estimated $\sigma$* — it converges to $z$ for
free, so there is never a reason to switch. The "30" folklore is just the point where the
difference becomes small enough that people stop caring.
:::

# The one-sample interval {#sec-one-sample}

We can now state and use the interval the whole article was built for. Because $T=(\bar
X_n-\mu)/(s/\sqrt n)\sim t_{n-1}$ is a pivot (@eq-t-pivot), run the [INFR-C1] recipe with
the $t$ quantile in place of the $z$ quantile. Let $t_{n-1,\alpha/2}$ be the value with
$P(T>t_{n-1,\alpha/2})=\alpha/2$. Because the $t_{n-1}$ density depends on $t$ only through
$t^2$ (the symmetry we read off @thm-t-density), it is an even function, so the lower tail
matches the upper: $P(T<-t_{n-1,\alpha/2})=\alpha/2$ as well. The two tails together carry
$\alpha$, leaving $P(-t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\le T\le t_{n-1,\alpha/2})=1-\alpha$. Substitute the
definition of $T$ and solve the inequality for $\mu$:
$$
-t_{n-1,\alpha/2} \;\le\; \frac{\bar X_n-\mu}{s/\sqrt n} \;\le\; t_{n-1,\alpha/2}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\bar X_n - t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt n}
\;\le\;\mu\;\le\;
\bar X_n + t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt n}.
$$
(Multiplying the chain by the positive quantity $s/\sqrt n$ preserves the inequalities;
then add $\bar X_n$ and flip signs, which reverses the chain back to the natural order.)
That is the boxed interval of the Core:

::: {#thm-t-interval}
**Theorem (one-sample $t$ interval).** For $X_1,\dots,X_n\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ i.i.d.
with $\mu,\sigma^2$ unknown, the random interval
$$
\Bigl[\,\bar X_n - t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\,\tfrac{s}{\sqrt n},\;\;
\bar X_n + t_{n-1,\alpha/2}\,\tfrac{s}{\sqrt n}\,\Bigr]
$$
has **exact** coverage $1-\alpha$: it contains $\mu$ with probability exactly $1-\alpha$,
for every $n\ge 2$ and every value of $\mu,\sigma^2$.
:::

"Exact" is the word to savor. Unlike the CLT-based Normal interval, which is only
*approximately* $1-\alpha$ and improves with $n$, the $t$ interval is *exactly* $1-\alpha$
at every sample size — *provided the data really are Normal*. That proviso is the entire
subject of @sec-failure.

## Worked example 1 — calibrating a lab instrument with $n=8$ {#sec-ex-lab}

A metrology lab certifies a reference mass and must report a $95\%$ confidence interval for
its true value from a handful of weighings on a precision balance. Repeated weighings of a
physical standard are about as Normal as real data get — the noise is a sum of many tiny
independent mechanical and thermal perturbations, the CLT's own habitat — but the lab can
afford only $n=8$ measurements per certification. This is the $t$'s home turf: genuinely
Normal, genuinely small, $\sigma$ genuinely unknown.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lab
#| fig-cap: "A reference mass measured $n=8$ times (deviations from nominal, in micrograms). **Left:** the measurements with the sample mean (green) and the $t$-based 95% interval (blue band). **Right:** the same interval compared with the naive Normal ($z$) interval — the $t$ interval is visibly wider, and that extra width is what buys honest 95% coverage at $n=8$."
# Eight weighings: deviation from nominal in micrograms.
y = np.array([12.4, 9.8, 11.1, 13.5, 10.2, 12.9, 8.7, 11.6])
n = len(y)
ybar = y.mean()
s = y.std(ddof=1)
se = s / np.sqrt(n)
tq = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=n - 1)
zq = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)
t_lo, t_hi = ybar - tq * se, ybar + tq * se
z_lo, z_hi = ybar - zq * se, ybar + zq * se

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(y, np.zeros_like(y), color=DATA, s=45, zorder=3, label="weighings")
axL.axvspan(t_lo, t_hi, color=DATA, alpha=0.18, label="95% $t$ interval")
axL.axvline(ybar, color=ACCENT, lw=2, label=fr"$\bar y={ybar:.2f}$")
axL.set_yticks([]); axL.set_xlabel("deviation from nominal ($\\mu$g)")
axL.set_title(f"$n={n}$ weighings"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8, loc="upper left")

axR.errorbar([0], [ybar], yerr=[[ybar - t_lo], [t_hi - ybar]], fmt="o", color=DATA,
             capsize=8, lw=2, label=f"$t$: [{t_lo:.2f}, {t_hi:.2f}]")
axR.errorbar([1], [ybar], yerr=[[ybar - z_lo], [z_hi - ybar]], fmt="o", color=NORMAL,
             capsize=8, lw=2, label=f"$z$: [{z_lo:.2f}, {z_hi:.2f}]")
axR.set_xlim(-0.6, 1.6); axR.set_xticks([0, 1]); axR.set_xticklabels(["$t$ interval", "$z$ interval"])
axR.set_ylabel("$\\mu$g"); axR.set_title("$t$ is wider (and honest)")
axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"ybar = {ybar:.3f},  s = {s:.3f},  se = s/sqrt(n) = {se:.3f}")
print(f"t_(7, .025) = {tq:.4f}   vs   z_.025 = {zq:.4f}")
print(f"95% t interval: [{t_lo:.3f}, {t_hi:.3f}]   half-width {tq*se:.3f}")
print(f"95% z interval: [{z_lo:.3f}, {z_hi:.3f}]   half-width {zq*se:.3f}")
```

Walk the numbers. The eight weighings average $\bar y=`{python} f"{ybar:.2f}"`\,\mu$g with
sample SD $s=`{python} f"{s:.2f}"`$, so the standard error is
$s/\sqrt n=`{python} f"{se:.3f}"`$. With $7$ degrees of freedom the $t$ quantile is
$t_{7,0.025}=`{python} f"{tq:.3f}"`$, not $1.96$. The half-width is
$`{python} f"{tq:.3f}"`\times`{python} f"{se:.3f}"`=`{python} f"{tq*se:.3f}"`$, giving
$\mu\in[`{python} f"{t_lo:.2f}"`, `{python} f"{t_hi:.2f}"`]$ micrograms with $95\%$
confidence. The naive $z$ interval would have been
$[`{python} f"{z_lo:.2f}"`, `{python} f"{z_hi:.2f}"`]$ — narrower by about
$`{python} f"{100*(tq-zq)/zq:.0f}"`\%$, and that extra narrowness is exactly the
overconfidence @fig-undercoverage warned about. Here the data are Normal, so the $t$
interval's $95\%$ is *exact*.

## Worked example 2 — a paired difference (the $t$'s most common real use) {#sec-ex-paired}

The single most common honest deployment of the one-sample $t$ is the **paired design**,
where each subject is measured twice and we analyze the *differences*. Pairing cancels
per-subject baseline variation, and the differences are usually far more Normal than the
raw measurements. Suppose a sleep study measures reaction time (ms) for $n=10$ participants
before and after a night of restricted sleep; we want a $95\%$ interval for the mean change.

```{python}
#| label: fig-paired
#| fig-cap: "A paired before/after design, $n=10$. **Left:** each participant's reaction time before (grey) and after (red) sleep restriction; lines connect the same person, so the analysis is on the within-person *differences*, which removes between-person baseline variation. **Right:** the 10 differences with the 95% one-sample $t$ interval for the mean change. The interval excludes zero, so the mean slowing is distinguishable from no effect at the 95% level."
before = np.array([248, 262, 271, 255, 239, 268, 277, 251, 244, 259.])
after  = np.array([263, 271, 290, 268, 250, 289, 295, 260, 261, 270.])
diff = after - before
n = len(diff)
dbar = diff.mean(); sd = diff.std(ddof=1); se = sd / np.sqrt(n)
tq = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=n - 1)
lo, hi = dbar - tq * se, dbar + tq * se

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
for b, a in zip(before, after):
    axL.plot([0, 1], [b, a], color=GREY, lw=1, alpha=0.7)
axL.scatter(np.zeros(n), before, color=GREY, s=35, zorder=3, label="before")
axL.scatter(np.ones(n), after, color=NORMAL, s=35, zorder=3, label="after")
axL.set_xlim(-0.4, 1.4); axL.set_xticks([0, 1]); axL.set_xticklabels(["before", "after"])
axL.set_ylabel("reaction time (ms)"); axL.set_title("Paired measurements")
axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

axR.scatter(diff, np.zeros(n), color=DATA, s=45, zorder=3)
axR.axvspan(lo, hi, color=DATA, alpha=0.18)
axR.axvline(dbar, color=ACCENT, lw=2, label=fr"$\bar d={dbar:.1f}$ ms")
axR.axvline(0, color=NORMAL, lw=1.5, ls="--", label="no change")
axR.set_yticks([]); axR.set_xlabel("change in reaction time (ms)")
axR.set_title(f"95% CI: [{lo:.1f}, {hi:.1f}]"); axR.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"mean difference dbar = {dbar:.2f} ms,  s_d = {sd:.2f},  se = {se:.3f}")
print(f"95% t interval for mean change: [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}] ms")
```

The mean slowing is $\bar d=`{python} f"{dbar:.1f}"`$ ms with a $95\%$ interval
$[`{python} f"{lo:.1f}"`, `{python} f"{hi:.1f}"`]$ ms. Because the interval lies entirely
above zero, "no change" is not a plausible value of the mean effect at the $95\%$ level —
this is the **duality between intervals and tests** that [INFR-D1] formalizes and that the
paired $t$-test in [INFR-D2] turns into an explicit decision. The reason this is a
*one*-sample problem despite "two" measurements per person: pairing reduces it to a single
column of differences, and we run the one-sample machinery on that column.

# Two samples: comparing two means {#sec-two-sample}

The everyday question is rarely "what is one mean" — it is "do two groups differ." Given an
independent sample $X_1,\dots,X_{n_1}\sim\mathcal N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)$ and
$Y_1,\dots,Y_{n_2}\sim\mathcal N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)$, we want an interval for the difference
$\mu_1-\mu_2$. The natural estimate is $\bar X-\bar Y$, and since the two samples are
independent, [INFR-A1]'s variance-of-a-difference rule gives
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X-\bar Y) = \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2},
$$ {#eq-two-var}
(variances of independent quantities add). With both $\sigma$'s unknown we again estimate
them and pay a $t$-style price — but *how* we estimate them forks the problem into two
classic cases.

## The equal-variance (pooled) case {#sec-pooled}

If we are willing to assume $\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2=\sigma^2$ (a common variance), then both
samples carry information about the *same* $\sigma^2$, and we should combine them. The
**pooled variance** is the degrees-of-freedom-weighted average of the two sample variances:
$$
s_p^2 = \frac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}.
$$ {#eq-pooled}
The logic is exactly the chi-square arithmetic of @sec-chisq. Each $(n_i-1)s_i^2/\sigma^2
\sim\chi^2_{n_i-1}$, and they are independent (different samples), so their sum is
$\chi^2_{(n_1-1)+(n_2-1)} = \chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}$ — *degrees of freedom add when independent
chi-squares add*, the same fact from @sec-chisq-def. Thus $(n_1+n_2-2)s_p^2/\sigma^2
\sim\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}$, and $s_p^2$ is the best pooled estimate of the shared $\sigma^2$
on $n_1+n_2-2$ degrees of freedom. Under equal variances @eq-two-var becomes
$\sigma^2(\tfrac{1}{n_1}+\tfrac{1}{n_2})$, so the pivot
$$
T = \frac{(\bar X-\bar Y)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)}{s_p\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n_1}+\tfrac{1}{n_2}}}
\;\sim\; t_{\,n_1+n_2-2}
$$ {#eq-pooled-t}
matches @def-t exactly. The **numerator**, standardized, is
$\bigl[(\bar X-\bar Y)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)\bigr]/\bigl(\sigma\sqrt{\tfrac1{n_1}+\tfrac1{n_2}}\bigr)
\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$, because $\bar X-\bar Y$ is Normal with mean $\mu_1-\mu_2$ and the
variance just computed. The **denominator** carries $s_p/\sigma=\sqrt{Q/(n_1+n_2-2)}$ with
$Q=(n_1+n_2-2)s_p^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}$. These two are independent, and we can
see exactly why without hand-waving: the numerator is a function of $(\bar X,\bar Y)$ only,
while $Q$ is a function of $(s_1^2,s_2^2)$ only. Within sample 1, $\bar X\perp s_1^2$ by
@thm-chisq; within sample 2, $\bar Y\perp s_2^2$; and the two samples are independent of
each other by assumption. So the pair $(\bar X,\bar Y)$ is independent of the pair
$(s_1^2,s_2^2)$, hence numerator $\perp$ denominator. Writing $T=Z/\sqrt{Q/(n_1+n_2-2)}$
with $Z\perp Q$ is precisely @def-t with $k=n_1+n_2-2$, so $T\sim t_{n_1+n_2-2}$ and the
$\sigma$ cancels just as in @eq-t-as-ratio. The interval is
$(\bar X-\bar Y)\pm t_{n_1+n_2-2,\alpha/2}\,s_p\sqrt{\tfrac1{n_1}+\tfrac1{n_2}}$.

## The unequal-variance case: the Welch correction {#sec-welch}

The pooled interval is *exact* — but only if the variances really are equal, which is a
strong and often false assumption. If $\sigma_1\ne\sigma_2$, pooling is wrong: it mixes a
small variance and a large one into a single number that describes neither group. The
robust default is **Welch's** approach, which estimates each variance separately and never
assumes they are equal. The statistic is
$$
T = \frac{(\bar X-\bar Y)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)}{\sqrt{\tfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\tfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}} .
$$ {#eq-welch-t}
Here is the honest subtlety, and it is worth stating plainly rather than hiding. The
denominator of @eq-welch-t is a sum of two *independent scaled chi-squares with different
scales* ($s_1^2/n_1$ and $s_2^2/n_2$). A weighted sum of chi-squares with unequal weights
is **not** itself a chi-square, so $T$ does **not** follow an exact $t$-distribution — there
is no finite $n$ at which @eq-welch-t is exactly $t$. This is a genuine theoretical wrinkle,
not a detail to wave away.

Welch's solution is an **approximation**: match the first two moments of the messy
denominator's distribution to those of a scaled chi-square, and use the degrees of freedom
that makes the match work. The result is the **Welch–Satterthwaite** degrees of freedom
$$
\nu = \frac{\bigl(\tfrac{s_1^2}{n_1}+\tfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}\bigr)^2}
{\dfrac{(s_1^2/n_1)^2}{n_1-1}+\dfrac{(s_2^2/n_2)^2}{n_2-1}},
$$ {#eq-welch-df}
which is generally **not an integer** and lies between $\min(n_1,n_2)-1$ and $n_1+n_2-2$.
The interval is then $(\bar X-\bar Y)\pm t_{\nu,\alpha/2}\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2}$,
treating $T\approx t_\nu$. The Satterthwaite moment-matching that produces @eq-welch-df is a
calculation in its own right; we flag it as a documented approximation rather than claim it
is exact, because it is not — its job is to make the *coverage* close to nominal, and it
does so remarkably well across unequal variances and unequal sample sizes.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Pooled or Welch — which should be the default?"* **Welch.** The pooled $t$ is exact under
equal variances but can be badly miscalibrated when they differ, especially with unequal
sample sizes (the classic failure: a small sample with large variance paired with a large
sample with small variance). Welch is only approximate but is robust to unequal variances
*and* reduces to essentially the pooled answer when the variances happen to match — so it
rarely costs anything and often saves you. Modern software (including R's `t.test`) defaults
to Welch for exactly this reason. The only argument for pooling is when you have strong
prior reason to believe the variances are equal *and* want the extra degrees of freedom in
a very small study; even then, check it. Do **not** select between them by first running a
variance-equality test on the same data — that two-stage procedure distorts the final
coverage.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-sample
#| fig-cap: 'Two-sample comparison of battery lifetimes (hours) for two manufacturing lots with clearly unequal spread ($n_1=9$, $n_2=7$). **Left:** the two groups; lot B is both lower and far more variable. **Right:** three 95% intervals for $\mu_A-\mu_B$ — pooled, Welch, and (for reference) a $z$ interval. The pooled interval is misleadingly narrow because it averages B''s large variance into A''s small one; Welch is wider and honest. All exclude zero, so the lots genuinely differ, but only Welch reports the uncertainty correctly.'
lotA = np.array([41.2, 40.8, 41.5, 40.9, 41.1, 41.3, 40.7, 41.4, 41.0])  # tight
lotB = np.array([38.1, 35.2, 40.3, 33.8, 39.9, 36.0, 41.2])              # spread
nA, nB = len(lotA), len(lotB)
mA, mB = lotA.mean(), lotB.mean()
sA2, sB2 = lotA.var(ddof=1), lotB.var(ddof=1)
diff = mA - mB

# pooled
sp2 = ((nA-1)*sA2 + (nB-1)*sB2) / (nA+nB-2)
se_pool = np.sqrt(sp2*(1/nA + 1/nB))
tq_pool = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=nA+nB-2)
# welch
se_w = np.sqrt(sA2/nA + sB2/nB)
nu = (sA2/nA + sB2/nB)**2 / ((sA2/nA)**2/(nA-1) + (sB2/nB)**2/(nB-1))
tq_w = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=nu)
# naive z
zq = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.8))
axL.scatter(np.full(nA, 0), lotA, color=DATA, s=40, zorder=3, label=f"lot A (s={np.sqrt(sA2):.2f})")
axL.scatter(np.full(nB, 1), lotB, color=NORMAL, s=40, zorder=3, label=f"lot B (s={np.sqrt(sB2):.2f})")
axL.set_xlim(-0.5, 1.5); axL.set_xticks([0, 1]); axL.set_xticklabels(["lot A", "lot B"])
axL.set_ylabel("lifetime (hours)"); axL.set_title("Unequal spread"); axL.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=8)

labels = ["pooled", f"Welch\n($\\nu$={nu:.1f})", "naive $z$"]
ses = [tq_pool*se_pool, tq_w*se_w, zq*se_w]
colors = [GREY, ACCENT, NORMAL]
for i, (hw, c) in enumerate(zip(ses, colors)):
    axR.errorbar([i], [diff], yerr=[[hw], [hw]], fmt="o", color=c, capsize=8, lw=2)
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.set_xticks([0, 1, 2]); axR.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=9)
axR.set_ylabel(r"$\mu_A-\mu_B$ (hours)"); axR.set_title("95% intervals for the difference")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"mean A={mA:.2f} (s^2={sA2:.3f}),  mean B={mB:.2f} (s^2={sB2:.3f})")
print(f"diff = {diff:.3f}")
print(f"pooled: t_df={nA+nB-2}, half-width {tq_pool*se_pool:.3f} -> [{diff-tq_pool*se_pool:.2f}, {diff+tq_pool*se_pool:.2f}]")
print(f"Welch : nu={nu:.2f},   half-width {tq_w*se_w:.3f} -> [{diff-tq_w*se_w:.2f}, {diff+tq_w*se_w:.2f}]")
```

The pooled and Welch half-widths differ noticeably here precisely because the variances are
so unequal; pooling B's large scatter with A's tight cluster understates the uncertainty in
the difference. Welch's $\nu=`{python} f"{nu:.1f}"`$ degrees of freedom — between the
groups' individual counts, and not an integer — is the price the approximation charges for
honesty.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The $t$ interval's exactness in @thm-t-interval rests on **one** load-bearing assumption:
the data are Normal. Drop it and "exact" becomes "approximate," sometimes badly so. This is
the section that separates "can compute a $t$ interval" from "knows when to trust one."

## Non-normal small samples: where coverage quietly fails {#sec-nonnormal}

The $t$-distribution accounts *exactly* for the randomness of $s$ **only when the
numerator is Normal and the chi-square structure of @thm-chisq holds** — both of which
require the underlying data to be Normal. For non-Normal data:

- The CLT [INFR-A2] still pulls $\bar X_n$ toward Normal as $n$ grows, so for *large* $n$
  the $t$ interval is approximately valid regardless of the data's shape (and the $t$
  quantile is nearly $z$ anyway). The $t$ machinery is asymptotically robust.
- But for *small* $n$ — exactly the regime where you reach for the $t$ — the CLT has not
  kicked in, and the interval's coverage can drift far from nominal. **Skewness is the
  killer.** When the data are skewed, $\bar X_n$ and $s$ become *correlated* (the
  independence of @sec-orthogonal fails off-Normal), and the interval systematically misses
  on one side: for right-skewed data the one-sample $t$ interval tends to sit too low and
  *undercovers*.

@fig-coverage-breakdown measures this honestly across distributions of increasing
non-Normality.

```{python}
#| label: fig-coverage-breakdown
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. For each of three source distributions — Normal (the assumption), a moderately skewed Exponential, and a heavy-skew Lognormal — we draw 30000 samples at each size $n$, build the nominal-95% one-sample $t$ interval, and record actual coverage. For Normal data coverage is exactly 95% at every $n$ (the theorem). For the skewed sources it is well below 95% at small $n$ — the Lognormal covers under 90% at $n=5$ — and only crawls back toward 95% as the CLT takes over. The $t$ interval's '95%' is a promise that holds for Normal data and degrades smoothly with skew."
dists = {
    "Normal":     lambda size: rng.standard_normal(size),
    "Exponential":lambda size: rng.exponential(1.0, size),
    "Lognormal":  lambda size: rng.lognormal(0.0, 1.0, size),
}
truth = {"Normal": 0.0, "Exponential": 1.0, "Lognormal": np.exp(0.5)}
ns = [5, 10, 20, 50, 100]
B = 30000
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for (name, draw), c in zip(dists.items(), [DATA, ACCENT, NORMAL]):
    covs = []
    mu = truth[name]
    for n in ns:
        S = draw((B, n))
        xbar = S.mean(axis=1); s = S.std(axis=1, ddof=1)
        half = stats.t.ppf(0.975, df=n-1) * s/np.sqrt(n)
        covs.append(np.mean(np.abs(xbar - mu) <= half))
    ax.plot(ns, covs, "o-", color=c, label=name)
ax.axhline(0.95, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.5, label="nominal 95%")
ax.set_xlabel("sample size $n$"); ax.set_ylabel("actual coverage of 95% $t$ interval")
ax.set_ylim(0.82, 0.97); ax.set_title("Non-normal small samples: coverage degrades with skew")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The Normal line sits dead on $0.95$ at every $n$ — the theorem made visible. The skewed
lines sag below it at small $n$, worst for the heavily skewed Lognormal, and recover only
as $n$ grows and the CLT rescues the numerator. The lesson is sharp: **the $t$ interval
inherits the Normal-data assumption from its very construction**, and when that assumption
fails in a small sample, no amount of using the "right" quantile fixes it, because the
*whole pivot* is mis-specified.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The $t$ does not make your data Normal — it assumes they already are.** A widespread
misconception treats the $t$-distribution as a fix for non-Normality ("use the $t$ for
small samples" as though the $t$ launders skew away). It does no such thing. The $t$ corrects
for one specific thing only: the **extra uncertainty from estimating $\sigma$**, *under the
assumption that the data are Normal*. If the data are skewed or heavy-tailed and $n$ is
small, the $t$ interval can quietly undercover, as @fig-coverage-breakdown shows. Always
look at the data (a histogram, a normal-quantile plot) before trusting a small-sample $t$
interval, exactly as you would always plot residuals before trusting a regression line.
:::

## Outliers and heavy tails: the denominator betrayed {#sec-outliers}

The $t$ interval depends on $\bar X_n$ *and* $s$, and **both** are non-robust. A single
gross outlier inflates $s$ (widening the interval) and shifts $\bar X_n$ (moving it) — and
because the interval's width is driven by $s$, one bad point can make the interval
uselessly wide or, if it drags $\bar X_n$ with it, point in the wrong place. Heavy-tailed
data (occasional huge values) do this routinely even without a data-entry error. The $t$ was
*derived* for Normal data, whose tails are thin; using it on genuinely heavy-tailed data
violates the premise.

## What you reach for instead {#sec-alternatives}

When the small-sample-and-non-Normal corner bites, the $t$ is the wrong tool. The honest
alternatives:

- **The bootstrap** ([INFR-E1], [INFR-E2]). Instead of assuming a Normal sampling
  distribution and looking up a $t$ quantile, *resample the data* to build the sampling
  distribution of $\bar X_n$ empirically — no distributional assumption at all. The
  bootstrap percentile and BCa intervals are the assumption-light replacements for the $t$
  interval, and [INFR-E2] compares their coverage against the $t$ on the very same kinds of
  skewed data as @fig-coverage-breakdown. This is the single most important pointer in the
  article: *when the $t$'s Normality assumption is the problem, the bootstrap is the answer.*
- **Permutation / rank methods** ([INFR-E3]) for two-sample comparisons, which assume only
  exchangeability under the null rather than Normality.
- **A transformation.** Right-skewed positive data (incomes, durations, concentrations) are
  often near-Normal on the log scale; a $t$ interval on $\log X$, back-transformed, can be
  far better calibrated — though it then estimates the mean of $\log X$ (a geometric-mean
  notion), not the mean of $X$, which is a different target you must own up to.
- **More data.** The cleanest fix when available: the CLT makes the $t$ interval
  asymptotically valid for *any* finite-variance distribution, so larger $n$ both validates
  the Normal approximation and shrinks the $t$-vs-$z$ gap to nothing.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the $t$ interval the right tool, and when is it the wrong one?"* The summary to be
able to recite:

- **Use it** when (a) you want an interval (or test) for a mean or a difference of means,
  (b) $\sigma$ is unknown and estimated from the sample (essentially always), and (c) the
  data are plausibly Normal *or* $n$ is large enough for the CLT. In its home regime —
  Normal data, small $n$, unknown $\sigma$ (Gosset's brewery) — it is *exactly* correct and
  unbeatable.
- **Do not lean on it** when $n$ is small *and* the data are clearly skewed or heavy-tailed
  (coverage degrades — use the bootstrap), when outliers dominate (the mean and $s$ are both
  corrupted — consider robust or rank methods), or when the two-sample variances differ
  (use Welch, not pooled).
- **The deepest point:** the $t$ buys you exactness for the price of a Normality assumption.
  The bootstrap buys you robustness for the price of needing enough data to resample
  meaningfully and giving up exactness. Knowing which price you can afford on a given dataset
  *is* the practitioner's judgment this track is training.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[INFR-C1 *Confidence Intervals*]**, which built coverage and the pivot
method and produced the known-$\sigma$ interval $\bar X_n\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/\sqrt n$.
This article removed the known-$\sigma$ fiction: it built the chi-square distribution of
$s^2$, assembled the $t$-distribution, and produced the honest small-sample interval
$\bar X_n\pm t_{n-1,\alpha/2}s/\sqrt n$. From here:

- **[INFR-C3 *Intervals From the Likelihood*]** generalizes interval construction beyond the
  mean to *any* MLE-estimated parameter (Wald, score, profile intervals), where the $t$ is
  one special, exact case of a broader asymptotic story.
- **[INFR-D1 *The Logic of Hypothesis Testing*]** formalizes the interval/test duality we
  used informally in @sec-ex-paired — every interval here has a test on the other side of it.
- **[INFR-D2 *The Workhorse Tests*]** is the immediate sequel: the one- and two-sample
  $t$-tests, the chi-square test, and the F-test are this exact machinery (the same $t$, the
  same $\chi^2$, and their ratio the F) *turned into a decision*. The chi-square distribution
  built in @sec-chisq and the pooled-variance arithmetic of @sec-pooled are consumed there
  directly.
- **[INFR-E2 *Bootstrap Confidence Intervals*]** is the assumption-light alternative this
  article kept pointing to: when the Normality premise fails in a small sample
  (@sec-failure), the bootstrap is what you reach for, and E2 races it against the $t$ on the
  same skewed data.

This article is also consumed by **Regression**: the $t$ interval and $t$ statistic are
exactly how a fitted coefficient's confidence interval and significance are reported
($\hat\beta\pm t_{\,\text{df}}\cdot\widehat{\operatorname{se}}(\hat\beta)$), with the
residual degrees of freedom playing the role of $n-1$.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The original.** "Student" [W. S. Gosset], "The Probable Error of a Mean,"
  *Biometrika* 6 (1908): 1–25 — the paper that introduced the distribution, written from the
  brewery's small-sample necessity. Worth reading for how concretely the small-$n$ problem
  is posed.
- **The rigorous derivation.** R. A. Fisher's geometric derivation (1925) recasts the $t$
  via the $n$-dimensional geometry of a Normal sample — the orthogonality argument of
  @sec-orthogonal is its descendant. Any mathematical-statistics text (Casella & Berger,
  *Statistical Inference*, Ch. 5) carries the modern treatment of @thm-chisq and
  @thm-t-density.
- **Welch's approximation.** B. L. Welch, "The generalization of 'Student's' problem when
  several different population variances are involved," *Biometrika* 34 (1947), and
  F. E. Satterthwaite's 1946 moment-matching note behind @eq-welch-df — the honest
  acknowledgment that the unequal-variance problem has no exact $t$ solution.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *Is $\bar X_n\pm t\,s/\sqrt n$ the best possible
  interval?* — it is the natural pivotal one and is exact under Normality, but likelihood-based
  intervals ([INFR-C3]) and the bootstrap ([INFR-E2]) generalize it in different directions.
  *What if even $s$ is a bad summary because the data are heavy-tailed?* — robust scale
  estimates and rank-based intervals, touched in [INFR-E3]. *Why does Normality, of all
  shapes, give the clean independence of $\bar X$ and $s^2$?* — it is essentially a
  characterization: the independence of the sample mean and sample variance holds *only* for
  the Normal (the Lukacs theorem), a deep fact left for the Probability track.
